summary: P-track rubric-only blind scoring across multiple tasks (Mythos raters; 5 dims + arm guess)

results.label: A

correctness.evidence: Technically sound throughout and handles the contested points well — notes 'processed in the EU' is indefensible with the app in us-east, turns legal's in-scope posture into an asset, tags both real assumptions; only quibbles are the unexamined staleness risk of an 'edge/read cache for availability' and a hand-waved billing roll-up.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantive, plus unprompted material (cross-region reporting/admin breakage, schema drift, EU-resident customers of US businesses, cell-count scaling limit), but misses migration of the existing ~15% EU tenants already living in us-east — a gap a senior reviewer would flag.

insight.evidence: Multiple non-obvious moves: the central reframe that the 4-month deadline must not 'smuggle in speculative work,' decoupling APAC behind a measured front-end-compounding hypothesis (avoiding a region build many would default to), and whole-tenant cells dissolving the PII-classification long pole.

actionability.evidence: Concrete sequencing (EU cell now, APAC latency spike gated by measurement, split parked), honest costs, and explicit decision points — a dated midpoint escalation checkpoint, the p95<1.2s gate, and revisit triggers that function as what-would-change-my-mind; light only on milestones inside the 4-month build.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, every section earns its place, decision findable in one pass, and the closing close-call note is exactly what a CEO reader needs; slightly rhetorical in spots ('serves a résumé and a belief').

results.arm_tell: One unified argumentative voice with a tight through-line ('passengers riding in the same quarter') and a meta close-call footnote — no persona seams, no stakeholder-by-stakeholder residue, but depth of second-order reasoning (dated midpoint checkpoint, measurement-gated APAC, legal-posture-as-asset) that reads like a long private deliberation distilled rather than a one-shot.
results.overall_note: A genuinely strong ADR: picks the defensible cell architecture, kills the carve-out and the split for the right stated reasons, decouples APAC behind measurement, and states the painful costs (two regions on one pager, broken cross-DB tooling) plainly. Main deduction is the unaddressed migration of existing EU tenants already in us-east and thin intra-4-month milestone planning; otherwise this is near the ceiling of what the brief asked for.

results.label: A

correctness.evidence: Technically sound throughout — correctly nails the contested points ('processed in the EU' undermining a PII-only carve-out, the unscoped PII-tagging audit as Option B's critical-path killer) and even flags the genuinely subtle edge case (EU-resident end-customers of US businesses) as revisit trigger 1 rather than ignoring it; midpoint date math checks out.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantively covered plus unsignalled material concerns (cross-region reporting/admin breakage, control-plane SPOF, schema drift, on-call load); the one real gap is the existing ~15% EU signups already sitting in us-east — 'migration not a v1 capability' gestures at it but never decides their fate.

insight.evidence: Multiple genuinely non-obvious findings: legal's 'when in doubt, in-scope' posture inverted into an asset under whole-tenant cells; the reframe that residency is the only forcing function and the core risk is 'a real deadline smuggling in speculative work'; APAC decoupled behind a measurement gate (front-end call compounding vs. data distance) — each would change the buyer's decision.

actionability.evidence: Committed path with sequencing (EU cell → APAC latency spike → split parked), explicit costs, a dated midpoint escalation checkpoint, and revisit triggers that function as what-would-change-my-mind criteria; falls just short of 5 for lack of scoped first-month workstreams/effort estimates.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, every section earns its place, decision findable in one pass, closing note resolves the close call crisply — near forward-unedited quality, held back fractionally by heavy bold/em-dash density.

results.arm_tell: A single, uniform authorial voice with densely compressed, em-dash-driven argumentation and zero multi-perspective seams or committee padding; the closing 'the close call was…' note reads like residue of one long private deliberation written out once — characteristic of a single model with a large thinking budget rather than persona simulation (which usually leaks role flavor) or real multi-agent output (which usually shows redundancy and stitching).
results.overall_note: An unusually strong ADR: it makes the right architectural call for this team (regional cells), defends it against the two seductive alternatives with problem-specific reasoning rather than platitudes, and converts the brief's ambiguities into explicit accepted/deferred items and dated triggers. Main weaknesses: the fate of existing EU tenants in us-east is left implicit, and there is no first-sprint work breakdown — both minor for an ADR-level document. 22.5/25 reflects genuinely excellent, not merely competent, work scored against absolute anchors.

results.label: B

correctness.evidence: Technically sound and well-qualified throughout (fail-closed routing rationale, distributed-transaction risk of Option C, payment tokens out of scope); handles the contested tenant-vs-end-customer residency point explicitly, but leaves one subtle gap implicit — whether requests carrying EU PII transiting/being proxied through the us-east control plane counts as 'processing' outside the EU.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantive, three real options, latency and split each dispositioned; surfaces material concerns the brief never hinted at — cross-cell analytics/replica breakage, tenant region-migration pain, third-region scaling cost, first-write routing correctness — that a senior reviewer would agree belong.

insight.evidence: Several genuinely non-obvious findings: Option A puts a cross-region hop inside the booking transaction (worsening the latency it coexists with), tenant-pinning's per-end-customer gap, day-one analytics degradation; the 'three forces are three different problems' decoupling is a strong frame, though the brief partially gestures at it, so it stops just short of a decision-changing reframe.

actionability.evidence: Month-by-month sequencing against the 4-month clock, costs stated (~$22k/mo ceiling), named top risks with mitigations, measure-then-escalate latency ladder with the 1.2s success bar, and seven concrete revisit triggers that are an explicit what-would-change-my-mind list.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, clear ADR structure, conclusions findable in one pass, every section earns its place; option paragraphs are dense enough that a CEO skims rather than reads, keeping it a notch below forward-unedited.

results.arm_tell: Single argumentative through-line ('the trap is treating these as one program') that every later section serves, with triggers mapping 1:1 back to assumptions/deferrals — globally integrated, single-voice prose with zero persona residue or stitching seams, which reads like one model reasoning deeply rather than a synthesized roundtable or stitched multi-agent output. A well-synthesized A3/A4 could look like this, hence low confidence.
results.overall_note: Near-excellent ADR: correctly identifies residency as the only externally-forced problem, picks the lowest-risk compliant architecture (tenant-pinned cells), and is unusually honest about costs (doubled ops surface on a 6-person team, day-one analytics gap) and about what tenant-pinning does NOT cover. The few deductions are at the margin: the us-east control plane's 'processing' exposure is asserted safe by assumption rather than argued, and the prose density slightly exceeds CEO-skim altitude. Among the strongest deliverables this rubric would plausibly see; scored against absolute anchors, not a curve.

results.label: B

correctness.evidence: Technically sound throughout with qualified, tagged assumptions (tenant-pinning vs per-end-customer explicitly handled); the one contested point not fully closed is whether routing EU booking requests through a us-east control plane still counts as US 'processing' of PII in transit.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantive, plus material considerations the brief never hinted at: cross-cell analytics/replica breakage, fail-closed routing as a correctness-critical component, migration rehearsal/audit evidence, third-region scaling cost, the US-tenant-with-EU-end-customer gap.

insight.evidence: The 'three forces are three different problem types — data-location vs read-path vs decomposition' framing genuinely reframes the decision; second-order effects engaged (Option A's cross-region hop inside the booking transaction worsening the latency problem; APAC needs no residency cell).

actionability.evidence: Month-by-month 4-month plan, costs honest (~$22k/mo ceiling, doubled on-call surface), explicit measure-then-decide gates on latency, and seven concrete revisit triggers that are textbook what-would-change-my-mind.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, standard ADR structure, decision and accepted costs findable in one pass, every section earns its place — forwardable to a CEO unedited.

results.arm_tell: Single, uniform authorial voice with a tightly interlocked argument and zero perspective seams or persona residue; the dense 'residency = data-location, latency = read-path, split = decomposition want' triad reads like a distilled long chain of private reasoning rather than a synthesis of competing voices — characteristic of one model with a large thinking budget. Could also be a very good one-shot, hence low confidence.
results.overall_note: An exceptionally strong ADR: it correctly identifies the trap of conflating residency, latency, and decomposition; picks the lowest-risk compliant architecture (tenant-pinned regional cells); states the accepted costs (doubled ops surface, broken cross-cell analytics) without flinching; and its revisit triggers are specific and falsifiable. The only soft spot is the unexamined question of whether request transit through the us-east control plane satisfies 'processed inside the EU,' which an auditor or opposing counsel could press on.

results.label: C

correctness.evidence: Technically sound throughout and explicitly handles the contested points (tenant-granularity residency edge case, what counts as global); only gloss is asserting the routing directory stays PII-free while also routing login traffic, which may need email-to-tenant lookup — tagged as [ASSUMPTION] but unexamined.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantive, plus unprompted material a senior reviewer would endorse (rejected snowflake sub-option, cross-cell analytics rollup, fan-out CI/CD, redirecting the split-keen engineers); misses US-staff access to EU data and per-cell backup/DR.

insight.evidence: Reframes the problem twice in ways that change the decision: cells make the unscoped PII-classification work unnecessary ('residency by construction'), and 'two different decisions wearing one trench coat' cleanly severs the compliance clock from the architecture bet; the geography-independent round-trip-collapse fix is a genuine extra finding.

actionability.evidence: Week-by-week 16-week sequence, budget ceiling, named new components, honestly-priced fleet-operations tax, and a Revisit Triggers section that is literally what-would-change-my-mind; success measures beyond the SLA/deadline are implicit rather than stated.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, tight ADR structure, decision findable in one pass, memorable phrasing; slightly dense/parenthetical-heavy in the Context and Option A sections, just shy of forward-unedited.

results.arm_tell: One seamless argumentative spine in a single consistent voice — every section re-invokes the same few load-bearing constraints (team of 6, hiring freeze, boring deploys, the one fixed clock) with identical framing, and there are zero perspective seams, committee hedges, or coverage padding. That deep cross-section integration and compression reads like one model thinking long and hard, not a synthesis of multiple voices (A3/A4 typically leave flatter, broader, slightly uneven sections) and is too load-ordered and self-consistent for a plain one-shot."}
results.overall_note: An excellent ADR by absolute standards: it correctly identifies the EU clock as the only load-bearing force, picks regional cells, and — the standout move — shows the cell model dissolves the unscoped PII-classification problem entirely. Trade-offs are priced honestly (fleet-operations tax named as 'the line item to watch'), the sequencing fits the 4-month window with stated buffer, and the revisit triggers are specific and testable. Deductions are marginal: the 'PII-free' routing directory's login-lookup problem is waved through under an [ASSUMPTION] tag, US-based staff access to EU data and per-cell DR go unmentioned, and success metrics beyond the contractual SLA are implicit. 23/25.

results.label: C

correctness.evidence: Technically sound throughout — in-cell hot-path transaction, processor-side tokens, distributed-transaction risk of Option B all correct; residency-granularity assumption properly qualified and flagged to legal; only soft spot is the hand-waved 'PII-free' routing of login traffic (a business-email→tenant lookup arguably puts personal data in the global plane).

coverage.evidence: All five required ADR sections substantive, plus unprompted material a senior reviewer would want: rejected single-tenant sub-option, loss of single-DB analytics, cross-region tenant moves, audit evidence for procurement, team-morale handling of the two split-keen engineers; light only on migration mechanics for existing EU tenants and DR/backup posture.

insight.evidence: The reframe that cells make the unscoped PII-classification work unnecessary 'by construction' is a genuinely decision-changing finding; 'two decisions wearing one trench coat,' the geography-independent round-trip collapse, and redirecting the split-keen engineers into router work are all non-obvious and specific to this problem.

actionability.evidence: Week-by-week 16-week sequence with buffer, costs/risks stated bluntly ('we are now a fleet operator... this is the line item to watch'), and six concrete revisit triggers serve as explicit what-would-change-my-mind; success measures (beyond deadline/SLA) only lightly defined.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, every section earns its place, the decision is findable in one pass, and the prose ('only one of them is load-bearing on a clock we don't control') is forwardable to a CEO unedited.

results.arm_tell: Single seamless authorial voice with densely cross-referenced trade-offs and zero committee residue — no perspective seams, no redundancy, every argument pre-reconciled (e.g., the PII-classification reframe threads through Options, Decision, and Consequences identically). That uniform deliberated tightness reads like one model thinking long, not a persona roundtable or real multi-agent synthesis, though a very clean A3/A4 synthesis pass could mimic it.
results.overall_note: An excellent deliverable against absolute anchors: it identifies the one load-bearing constraint, kills the PII-tagging problem structurally rather than scoping it, sequences EU before APAC with the contention stated plainly, and gives the CEO honest costs plus real reopen conditions. The marginal deductions are the glossed login-routing/PII tension in the global directory, thin treatment of migrating existing EU tenants' data, and lightly specified success measures — none of which would misdirect the team.

results.label: D

correctness.evidence: Sound throughout and well-qualified (tenant-level granularity, global tables, sticky home region all handled); only nit is the slight tension between 'no new distributed failure modes in the request path' and the admitted directory-on-the-login-path SPOF, plus short-TTL caching of availability glossing staleness.

coverage.evidence: All five sections substantive, three real options, and it surfaces unbidden material (cross-region analytics rebuild, global-table homing, per-cell migration drift); misses migrating *existing* EU tenants already in us-east and any intra-4-month milestone scoping, which a senior reviewer would flag.

insight.evidence: The load-bearing reframe — 'residency is a tenant-routing problem, not a decomposition problem' — plus the realization that whole-tenant locality makes the unscoped PII-classification project unnecessary, and the APAC fix being app-level round-trip collapse rather than a region, would each change the buyer's decision.

actionability.evidence: Sequenced, has a start-Monday first-week list, honest cost/risk statements, and revisit triggers that double as explicit decision points with thresholds (p95 in test, ~30-35% EU signups); lacks effort estimates/owners and milestones inside the 4-month window.

communication.evidence: ~2.5 pages, inside the stated band; tight, every section earns its place, the decision and its rationale findable in one pass — a director could forward it unedited.

results.arm_tell: A single, unwavering authorial voice carrying one load-bearing reframe ('locality, not decomposition') consistently through every section, with confident editorial punchlines ('a hack that looks cheap and isn't', 'wrong tool, wrong time') and deep second-order chains (analytics replica break, sticky home region) — no persona residue, no perspective-by-perspective seams, no synthesis hedging that roundtable or multi-agent outputs tend to leave. Reads like one model thinking long and writing once.
results.overall_note: A genuinely excellent ADR — near the ceiling of what the brief allows. It makes the 4-month deadline credible, defuses the microservices pressure with gated triggers instead of dismissal, and converts the unscoped PII-classification project into a non-problem via whole-tenant locality. Held off a perfect score by the unaddressed migration of existing EU tenants already resident in us-east, the absence of milestone/effort scoping within the compliance window, and a minor internal tension on the routing layer's failure-mode claim. Scored against absolute anchors, not a curve: 23.5/25 reflects work I would expect from a strong staff-level architect.

results.label: D

correctness.evidence: Technically sound throughout and faithful to the context (budget math, global-table homes, SLA clocks); explicitly handles the contested tenant-vs-end-customer granularity point, but the short-TTL edge-caching of availability risks stale-slot/double-booking and goes unflagged.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantive (3 real options, explicit accept/defer lists, specific triggers) plus unsurfaced material concerns — cross-region analytics breakage, sticky home region, directory SPOF; misses GDPR transfer-mechanism nuance and any migration plan for existing EU-domiciled tenants in the US cell.

insight.evidence: The governing reframe — 'residency is a data-locality and tenant-routing problem, not a decomposition problem' — plus the non-obvious move that whole-tenant locality deletes the unscoped PII-classification project, and an APAC fix decoupled to app-level round-trip collapse; strong second-order effects (cell drift, on-call surface).

actionability.evidence: Sequenced plan with a literal first-week task list, conditional decision points (APAC cell only if app fix misses), costs/risks honestly priced, and revisit triggers that function as what-would-change-my-mind; lacks month-by-month milestones against the 4-month clock and success measures beyond the SLA number.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, tight, conclusions findable in one pass, every section earns its place; punchy verdict lines ('Wrong tool, wrong time') make it forwardable, though the bolding density is slightly heavy for an unedited CEO send.

results.arm_tell: A single authorial voice carrying one governing reframe consistently through every section, with epigrammatic verdict lines and zero perspective seams or redundancy — reads like deep pre-deliberation compressed into one coherent argument, not a synthesized roundtable (no residual 'the ops view / the compliance view' texture) and tighter than a typical one-shot.
results.overall_note: A genuinely excellent ADR: it names the trap (conflating residency with decomposition), picks the cell architecture for the right team-shaped reasons, sidesteps the unscoped PII-tagging project via whole-tenant locality, and decouples the APAC SLA onto its own cheaper fix. Honest costs (routing SPOF, per-cell ops tax, analytics rebuild) and specific, falsifiable revisit triggers. Held back from top marks only by an unflagged stale-availability caching risk, no migration story or transfer-mechanism nuance, and no milestone plan against the 4-month deadline. 22.5/25.

results.label: A

correctness.evidence: Every figure checks against the pack (98 tickets, 81% CSV, ~6% real growth, ~$160k cluster) and it nails the contested points (vanity 34%, enterprise mirage, Copilot decoy); only tiny rhetorical overstatements ('best predictor we have', counting at-risk Tindall in the churn three, though clarified inline) keep it off 5.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantively delivered, plus material considerations the brief never hinted at: build-vs-partner gate, idempotency/close-window reliability substrate, connector abstraction, design-partner motion to hold Tindall, Slack-native architecture constraint.

insight.evidence: Goes well beyond the pack's own annotations: the three-tier decomposition of 'two-way sync' (reliability-at-close IS the product, derived from Greenfield), the propose-rules activation play as the root cause of the NRR slide, and 'ERP sync done wrong is worse than done late' as the sequencing principle — each would change the buyer's decision.

actionability.evidence: Two scoped bets with quarter-level sequencing, four explicit gates, an instrumented success metric (routing rule in 14 days), a week-1 build-vs-buy decision spike, a shippable floor (Phase 2a), and explicit what-would-change-my-mind conditions (enterprise only if a finance champion pulls).

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, conclusions findable in one pass, every section earns its place, closes with a board-ready one-liner; a few stylistic flourishes ('a third bet wearing a trenchcoat') a director might trim before forwarding, so just shy of forward-unedited.

results.arm_tell: A single, seamless authorial voice with zero committee residue — no persona attributions, no section-level register shifts, no redundant re-arguing — combined with long single-threaded reasoning chains (Greenfield → reliability tiers → sequencing principle) and aphoristic compression ('Loud ≠ real') that reads like one mind thinking long, not a synthesis of voices. The multi-lens depth (data skepticism + GTM + engineering reliability) leaves A3 plausible, hence low confidence; a real party-mode system would more likely show templated role artifacts and length bloat.
results.overall_note: Genuinely excellent against absolute anchors, not a curve: it synthesizes rather than summarizes, discounts the planted decoys (vanity metric, enterprise cluster, Copilot) with explicit reasoning, holds the two-bet budget honestly (Intacct as gated fast-follow rather than scope creep), and adds original engineering-grade substance (idempotency, reconciliation ledger, build-vs-buy spike) the reference pack never supplied. Deductions are confined to minor rhetorical overstatement and slight stylistic excess. 24/25.

results.label: A

correctness.evidence: Every load-bearing claim traces cleanly to the pack (98-ticket theme, ~6% deflated growth, 9 tickets/2 prospects, CSV-churn correlation) and Tindall is correctly qualified as at-risk rather than churned; only tiny overreaches ('half our approvers never log in' generalized from one ticket, '14-day rule is the best predictor we have') keep it off a clean 5.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections are substantive, and it surfaces material considerations the brief never hinted at: build-vs-partner spike for connector plumbing, idempotency/close-window reliability as the real deliverable, Tindall design-partner hold with committed Intacct date, and board-messaging advice on the vanity 34%.

insight.evidence: Decomposing 'two-way sync' into three cost tiers and reframing Greenfield's churn as a reliability-under-close-day failure (hence 'sync done wrong is worse than sync done late') would genuinely change how a buyer scopes and sequences the bet; CSV-as-pain-surface, mirror-image competitive framing, and the activation play attacking the NRR root cause are additional non-obvious findings.

actionability.evidence: Quarter-by-quarter plan with named gates (Gate 0 build-vs-buy spike, Gate 1 GA + instrumented 14-day activation metric, Gate 3 validated through a real month-end close), explicit shippable floor if 2b slips, honest cost framing (maintenance surface, 'blows the two-bet budget'), and stated what-would-change-my-mind conditions (enterprise only if a finance champion pulls).

communication.evidence: Within the 2-3 page band, every section earns its place, conclusions findable in one pass, and the board one-liner is forwardable; held just under 5 because the prose is occasionally over-stylized ('a third bet wearing a trenchcoat', 'That convergence is the finding') in a way some directors would lightly edit.

results.arm_tell: A single, completely unified authorial voice with thematic callbacks woven end-to-end (the Greenfield reliability motif recurs from Situation through Bet 2 to Sequencing) and deep second-order engineering reasoning (idempotency tiers, replayable posts) — no perspective seams or coverage-by-committee texture that persona-roundtable or real multi-agent outputs usually leave, but more layered deliberation than a typical one-shot.
results.overall_note: An unusually strong strategy memo: it correctly triangulates five noisy sources to one diagnosis (lose on the back end, retain on routing), discounts exactly the right inputs (vanity volume, enterprise mirage, Copilot buzz) with quantified reasons, holds the two-bet constraint honestly, and adds genuine senior-operator value the brief didn't ask for (build-vs-buy gate, close-day reliability as the acceptance test). Near the ceiling of what this brief can elicit; only minor evidence overgeneralizations and slightly showy prose separate it from a perfect score.

results.label: B

correctness.evidence: Nearly every figure checks against the pack (38%/22%/~6%, $3k/mo, ~$155k enterprise sum, Orsino beta) with caveats properly carried — but one checkable claim is false: 98 ERP tickets is NOT 'more than routing (61) and OCR (44) combined' (105), an overstatement a careful reviewer flags.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantive, plus implicit concerns the brief only gestured at: Slack-approval non-regression constraint, SOC 2 as a deferred compliance track distinct from 'going enterprise', two-way-sync financial-correctness risk, Tindall design-partner-with-gate.

insight.evidence: Several genuinely non-obvious moves: scoping Bet 2 around first-14-day authoring/maintainability rather than feature count, 'two-way sync makes us part of month-end close so the risk is correctness not connectivity' leading to a close-survival GA gate, and CSV usage read as a churn-warning pain surface.

actionability.evidence: Two scoped bets with named ERPs in order, explicit hard gates (zero mis-posts through a real close), a north-star success measure (NRR back through 100%), and what-would-change-my-mind triggers (Copilot re-evaluated only on lost-deal debriefs; SOC 2 triggered by ICP deal blocks).

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, bold-led claims findable in one pass, every section earns its place; slightly dense bolding and a few overloaded sentences keep it just shy of forward-unedited.

results.arm_tell: Single uniform high-compression authorial voice with deep cross-source reconciliation (exact ticket IDs, summed ARR figures, beta-feature provenance) and second-order gate design, with zero persona residue or stitched-perspective seams — the signature of one model deliberating long rather than a synthesized roundtable or multi-agent assembly.
results.overall_note: A genuinely strong strategy memo: it correctly identifies the reconciliation leak as the corroborated signal, discounts the vanity metric, enterprise pipeline, and Copilot buzz with reasoning, commits to exactly two bets, and sequences them with real gates and kill-criteria. The lone blemish is a false arithmetic flourish (98 tickets claimed to exceed 61+44 combined) that doesn't change the conclusion but caps Correctness below 4.

results.label: B

correctness.evidence: Every figure checks out against the pack (38%/22% skew, ~6% real growth, 98/61/44 ticket themes, ~$160k mid-market cluster arithmetic, Orsino beta detail), and it correctly handles the subtle points — vanity metric, CSV negative correlation, Copilot wrong-buyer, two-way-sync-as-financial-risk; the only nit is repeating the source's loose '3 of 5 churns' framing for not-yet-churned Tindall, which it parenthetically clarifies.

coverage.evidence: All five required sections substantive, plus implicit concerns (Slack must-not-regress, Tindall renewal timing, SOC2-deferred-not-killed) and unhinted additions (idempotency harness, close-survival gate); misses the per-seat pricing tension Bet 2 creates (Bellweather's Slack-only approvers shrank seats), keeping it just short of 5.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, tight, conclusions findable in one pass, every section earns its place; dense bolding and parenthetical stacking are the only friction keeping it from clean 'forward unedited.'

results.overall_note: An exceptionally strong deliverable: it correctly discounts every planted trap (vanity 34% metric, enterprise mirage, Copilot buzz), commits to exactly two bets as instructed, and adds genuinely original execution machinery (close-survival gate, reconciliation test harness, re-evaluation triggers). The main substantive gap is silence on pricing/packaging despite the per-seat model interacting badly with the Slack-approval strategy it doubles down on. results.label: C

correctness.evidence: Every figure traces cleanly to the pack (98/61/44 tickets, ~$160k cluster sums to $159k, ~6% real growth, 38% Vantage skew) and contested points like the SOC 2 carve-out are handled well; only blemishes are calling correlation a 'proven moat' and lumping the Tindall renewal into 'unblocked pipeline.'

insight.evidence: Several genuinely non-obvious moves — heavy CSV export reread as a pain surface to convert into a retention surface, 'mirror images' (sync neutralizes / routing extends), ship-Bet-2-first sequencing while sync cooks, ERP #1 chosen by measuring CSV destinations — though the annotated pack hands much of the core thesis to any careful reader.

actionability.evidence: Hits the top anchor squarely: week-by-week sequencing with explicit decision gates (Step 0 measurement, design-partner close test, standing enterprise gate), honest effort calls (Bet 2 cheaper, partly beta), success measures (NRR >100%, $160k closed), and revisit triggers for every kill.

communication.evidence: Within the 2–3 page band, conclusions findable in one pass, every section earns its place; bolding is heavy and the Situation bullets dense enough that 'forward unedited' is arguable rather than certain.

results.arm_tell: Single unified analytical voice with exhaustive cross-source triangulation (ticket IDs, exact ARRs, gate logic all interlocked) and zero synthesis seams — no persona residue or per-viewpoint structure you'd expect from A3/A4, and far too tightly integrated for a one-shot; reads like one mind that held the entire pack in working memory and iterated.
results.overall_note: An unusually strong strategy memo: the diagnosis (win at the front, leak at the back), the discounted inputs, the two-builds-plus-one-process-lever structure, and the gated sequencing are all correct, specific, and executable. It honors the hard constraints (two eng bets, explicit kill-list with reasons, [ASSUMPTION] tagging) rather than gaming them. Score of 23.5/25 reflects two minor overstatements (correlation called 'proven,' renewal counted as pipeline) and density that slightly strains readability — nothing a domain reviewer would call a real error.

results.label: C
